domain_knowledge: Contrastive learning maximizes agreement between representations of augmented views of the same sample while pushing apart representations from different samples, preventing the encoder from learning trivial constant solutions.
A Projection module typically maps high-dimensional encoder outputs to a lower-dimensional space via a learnable MLP, making the contrastive loss computation more effective and stable.

A Prediction module adds an additional MLP head after projection, creating asymmetry between the two branches (stop-gradient on one path) to prevent trivial convergence where the network learns identical representations for all inputs.

For ion image data in mass spectrometry, preventing representation collapse is critical because collapsed embeddings lose discriminative power needed to distinguish co-localized ions (COL mode) or isotope relationships (ISO mode).

Contrastive loss functions (e.g., NT-Xent, SimCLR) require careful temperature scaling and batch composition to balance positive pair attraction against negative pair repulsion without dominating gradients.

script_runs: execute the contrastive loss computation on paired augmented representations and confirm output is a scalar loss value
expert_review: assess whether the Projection and Prediction module architecture design follows established contrastive learning best practices (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL) to avoid solution collapse
assess whether the contrastive loss formulation (e.g., NT-Xent, cosine similarity weighting) is appropriate for ion image representation learning
assess whether the integration of Projection and Prediction modules with the ResNet18 encoder outputs is mathematically and computationally sound

cmd: import torch; import torch.nn as nn; from torchvision.models import resnet18; print('Task 003: Projection and Prediction modules with contrastive loss'); encoder = resnet18(pretrained=False); print('ResNet18 encoder loaded'); projection = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(512, 2048), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(2048, 128)); prediction = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(128, 2048), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(2048, 128)); print('Projection and Prediction modules initialized'); print('Modules ready for contrastive training on encoder outputs from:', '{inputs.encoder_vectors}'); print('Augmented images available from:', '{inputs.augmented_images}')

methodology_summary: Load 512-dimensional encoder outputs from paired ResNet18 networks processing augmented ion image pairs.
Pass encoder outputs through a learnable Projection module to map to a contrastive learning space.
Pass projected vectors through a learnable Prediction module to introduce asymmetry and prevent solution collapse.
Compute contrastive loss (e.g., NT-Xent or SimCLR loss) between predictions of augmented pairs, maximizing similarity within pairs and minimizing across pairs.
Optimize Projection and Prediction module parameters via backpropagation while maintaining encoder representations.

Validation: contrastive loss converges to a stable minimum and learned representations preserve ion-image-specific structure (verified by downstream clustering or classification performance on COL/ISO modes).

workflow_description: 1. Load 512-dimensional representation vectors output from the paired ResNet18 encoders for augmented ion image pairs. 2. Apply the Projection module to transform encoder outputs into a lower-dimensional projection space suitable for contrastive learning. 3. Apply the Prediction module to generate prediction outputs from the projection space, introducing an asymmetry to avoid trivial solutions. 4. Compute contrastive loss between projections/predictions of augmented image pairs, maximizing similarity for the same ion image while minimizing similarity across different images. 5. Backpropagate loss through the Projection and Prediction modules to update their learnable parameters while keeping encoder weights frozen or jointly optimized as specified.
